keyTakeaway: Formula: **∫x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C**. Apply directly to your polynomial coefficients to find its indefinite integral using the power rule.

explanation:
**Mathematics** is a universal tool: algebra, geometry, calculus, and statistics are applied across engineering, science, finance, and everyday life.

## How It Works

This calculator applies the power rule of integration to polynomial coefficients. Simply enter your coefficients in descending order of degree, and we'll compute the indefinite integral instantly and free.

The calculator processes your input against the standard integration formula, checks for typical ranges, and alerts you if values seem unusual.

## Important Notes

This calculation is provided for reference purposes. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult with a qualified professional. Our information is current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly to ensure accuracy.

faq.q: What is the power rule for integration?
faq.a: ∫x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C. Apply this formula to each term of your polynomial.

faq.q: Why do we always add C?
faq.a: C is the constant of integration, representing all possible antiderivatives of your function.
An antiderivative without bounds—it includes the constant C and represents a family of functions.

faq.q: How do I integrate a polynomial?
faq.a: Apply the power rule to each term separately, then combine them and add C.
∫u·dv = uv - ∫v·du. Use this when you have products of functions.
